findings.description: Header/letterhead information is duplicated in the HTML. The officials' names and department name appear once as a visual header at the top of the page, then again as inline text content after a footer separator and colored bar, before the MEMORANDUM heading begins.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 shows the letterhead (PAT McCRORY/Governor, DONALD R. VAN DER VAART/Secretary, TRACY DAVIS/Director, Energy Mineral & Land Resources) once in the header area. HTML chunk 1 renders this header visually at the top, then inserts footer text and a colored bar, then repeats the same letterhead information as body text before the MEMORANDUM heading. A reader encounters the same identifying information twice with a footer in between.

findings.description: Footer text and a colored page-separator bar are inserted between page-equivalent content sections in the continuous HTML, specifically between the end of the memo outline (PDF page 1 content) and the beginning of Section I History/Background (PDF page 2 content).
findings.evidence: In HTML chunk 2, after the outline items I–IV, there is a footer ('State of North Carolina | Environmental Quality…') and a colored bar before the 'I. History/Background' section begins. This interleaving of footer material between content sections disrupts continuous reading order.

findings.description: A decorative page-footer/background image from the PDF's page 1 is inserted mid-content in the HTML body, between the Table of Contents navigation and Section I (History/Background), breaking reading flow.
findings.evidence:
HTML contains placed between the closing the TOC and the

heading. This is a per-page decorative element from the PDF footer area, not substantive body content.

findings.description: Generic placeholder alt text on a decorative image inserted mid-content. The alt text 'Page background graphic' contains the generic term 'graphic' and provides no meaningful information about the image content.
findings.evidence: HTML: . The word 'graphic' is explicitly listed as a generic placeholder term.
